:::{#def-ph-first-order-interval-fail-prob}
#### Proportional-hazards first-order interval failure probability

Under the proportional-hazards model (@thm-ph-haz-decomp),
the [first-order interval failure probability](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-first-order-interval-fail-prob)
factors into a shared baseline term and a subject-specific hazard ratio:

$$
q^*_j(\tau) = \dblue{\haz_0(\tau)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}.
$$
:::

:::{#exm-ph-first-order-interval-fail-prob}
#### First-order approximation under the PH model

Take a half-year interval $[4.0,\, 4.5)$ with $\Delta t = 0.5$ yr,
baseline hazard $\dblue{\haz_0(4.0) = 0.1}$/yr,
and a subject with hazard ratio $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)} = 2$.
The proportional-hazards factorization splits the first-order probability
into the shared baseline factor and the subject's hazard ratio:

$$
q^*_j(4.0)
= \dblue{\haz_0(4.0)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}
= \dblue{(0.1 \times 0.5)}\;\red{2}
= \dblue{0.05}\;\red{2}
= 0.10 .
$$

Equivalently, the subject's total hazard is
$\haz(4.0 \mid \vx_j) = \dblue{\haz_0(4.0)}\,\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)} = 0.2$/yr,
so $q^*_j(4.0) = \haz(4.0 \mid \vx_j)\,\Delta t = 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.10$.
:::
